description: Create a regional outlook content slide with a two-column layout showing base case outlook and what to watch for by country across the Americas and Middle East.

header.headline: Unwinding of OPEC production cuts will help GCC countries maintain strong growth in 2026, whereas Canada, Mexico and Brazil remain on shakier footing due to US/China exposures

items.text: Americas — Canada: Slight improvement vs. 2025 as the drag from past rate hikes fades, though growth remains US demand-dependent and pressured by tariffs. Structural challenges—productivity, housing supply, limited fiscal room—keep Canada in the lower-growth tier of the global multi-speed landscape.

Americas — Brazil: Decelerating overall growth as tighter financial conditions and high real rates weigh on domestic investment and consumer demand. Weaker global goods trade and China’s export overhang will also pressure Brazil’s manufacturing and commodity-linked sectors.

Americas — Mexico: Slight growth pickup supported by nearshoring, remittances, and a firm labor market, but still constrained by weak investment, softer US/global demand, and tariff headwinds. Tight financial/fiscal conditions—high real rates, limited fiscal room, energy-sector bottlenecks—will temper benefits of disinflation.

Middle East — Saudi Arabia: Slight acceleration of growth as oil production normalizes from prior OPEC+ cuts and non-oil activity remains resilient on the back of steady investment, job creation, and low inflation. Momentum is partly tempered by higher living costs (housing), subsidy adjustments, and a more cautious fiscal stance.
Middle East — UAE: Growth is expected to moderate slightly from 2025’s strong pace as the multi-year non-oil surge continues to normalize.

Canada: How quickly monetary easing transmits through a debt-heavy household sector; outcome of USMCA renegotiations and any easing in tariffs; signs of housing-market stabilization vs. renewed stress.
Brazil: Interaction between rate cuts and inflation expectations amid currency volatility; exposure to China’s demand for metals, agri., and energy; execution of fiscal reforms and market reactions.

Mexico: USMCA review and tariff decisions shaping nearshoring economics and export competitiveness; Banxico’s easing path and effects on credit and peso volatility; how fiscal and energy-sector signals (Pemex risks, power reliability, budget plans) affect investor confidence.

Saudi Arabia: Oil prices and OPEC+ quota decisions, the main swing factor for headline growth and fiscal space; translation of committed public and private investments into actual project delivery and non-oil activity; impact of subsidy reforms, housing costs, and public-sector wage dynamics on consumer sentiment.
UAE: Watch factors not fully visible in the provided HTML snippet.
